channel.title: NYT > Theater
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Tonys 2026 Highlights: ‘Schmigadoon!’ and ‘Death of a Salesman’ Are Big Winners
item.description: “Ragtime” won best musical revival, while “Death of a Salesman” led with six prizes. Joshua Henry, Caissie Levy, John Lithgow and Laurie Metcalf were among the winners.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Our Favorite Photos From the Tony Awards
item.description: As Broadway’s brightest gathered for the industry’s biggest night, here’s an up-close look at what you couldn’t see on TV.
item.dc:creator: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet
item.media:description: Lesley Manville after she won for her performance in “Oedipus” on Sunday night.

item.title: Tony Award Winners 2026: See the Full List
item.description: The Tony Awards were held at Radio City Music Hall in New York City.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: John Lithgow, the star of “Giant,” with his award for best actor in a play.

item.title: 2026 Tony Awards Predictions: Who Will Win?
item.description: Expect wins for the musical “Schmigadoon!” and the play “Liberation,” and for the “Ragtime” stars Joshua Henry and Caissie Levy.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)

item.title: Caissie Levy, Tony Awards Contender and ‘Ragtime’ Star, Is Having a Moment
item.description: Caissie Levy was Broadway’s first Elsa. She starred in “Hair” and “Ghost.” And now, for “Ragtime,” she is an odds-on favorite to win a Tony Award.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and George Etheredge
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times

item.title: 38 Tony Nominees Reveal the Strangest Skills They’ve Picked Up
item.description: The stars of “Giant,” “Fallen Angels,” “The Rocky Horror Show,” “Ragtime” and more prove they’ll go to great lengths to be believable in a role.
item.dc:creator: Dana Scruggs, Michael Paulson, Alexis Soloski, Nicole Herrington, Jolie Ruben and Amanda Webster
item.media:credit: Dana Scruggs for The New York Times

item.title: Nathan Lane and Laurie Metcalf on Their Tony-Nominated ‘Death of a Salesman’
item.description: The stars Nathan Lane and Laurie Metcalf talk with the show’s director, Joe Mantello, about the exhilaration of collaborating and the trap of sentimentality.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.media:credit: Ye Fan for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Nathan Lane, Joe Mantello and Laurie Metcalf in Lane’s dressing room at the Winter Garden Theater in Manhattan after a recent performance of “Death of a Salesman.”

item.title: Mark and Joaquin Consuelos, Father and Son, Make Broadway Debuts Together
item.description: This spring the talk-show host and his youngest child made simultaneous debuts, three weeks and eight blocks apart.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and OK McCausland
item.media:description: “I’m already sad that it’s going to be over,” Joaquin Consuelos said. “And now I want to do everything. I don’t want to limit myself.”

item.title: In ‘Can I Be Frank?,’ Imitation Is the Sincerest Form of Tragedy
item.description: Morgan Bassichis, whose solo show “Can I Be Frank?” resurrects an act by Frank Maya, joins others this season who are recreating the works of deceased artists.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: Morgan Bassichis in the solo show “Can I Be Frank?” at the SoHo Playhouse in Manhattan.

item.title: ‘Girl, Interrupted’ Review: A Musical That’s a Little Too Tranquilized
item.description: While the 1999 movie went for melodrama, this stage adaptation with songs by Aimee Mann honors the memoir’s coolly clinical prose.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: The cast of “Girl, Interrupted” at the Public Theater in Manhattan, from left: Mia Pak, Sally Shaw, Juliana Canfield, Katherine Reis, Ta’Rea Campbell and King Princess.

item.title: Rébecca Chaillon Punished Her Body Onstage for Years. Now, She’s Testing New Limits.
item.description: Rébecca Chaillon’s latest show tackles social stigma by featuring only performers she describes as “fat” — a label she also applies to herself.
item.dc:creator: Laura Cappelle
item.media:credit: Elliott Verdier for The New York Times
item.media:description: “No one is going to hurt themselves like you do,” a member of the production team for Rébecca Chaillon’s new show told her.

item.title: At the Tonys, calls in support of trans rights and free speech.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor

item.title: ‘Schmigadoon!’ Wins Best Musical Tony Award
item.description: The knowing, and loving, musical theater satire won the coveted award of the night.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: A scene from Broadway’s “Schmigadoon!,” which is running at the Nederlander Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Joshua Henry Wins the Tony for Best Actor in a Musical.
item.description: As Coalhouse Walker Jr. in “Ragtime,” Henry belts out emotional solos that have prompted mid-show standing ovations.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Joshua Henry, who plays Coalhouse Walker Jr. in the revival of “Ragtime.”

item.title: Caissie Levy wins the Tony for best actress in a musical.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: From left: Joshua Henry, Caissie Levy and Brandon Uranowitz in “Ragtime.”

item.title: ‘Death of a Salesman’ Wins the Tony for Best Revival of a Play
item.description: Arthur Miller’s classic won the same award in 1984, 1999 and 2012.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.media:description: Laurie Metcalf, left, as Linda and Nathan Lane as Willy Loman in the Arthur Miller play “Death of a Salesman” at the Winter Garden Theater in New York.

item.title: ‘Ragtime’ Wins the Tony for Best Revival of a Musical
item.description: Based on the novel by E.L. Doctorow, “Ragtime” follows three communities colliding in turn-of-the-20th-century New York.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: Nichelle Lewis, left, as Sarah and Joshua Henry as Coalhouse Walker Jr. in the musical “Ragtime.”

item.title: Lesley Manville wins the Tony for best actress in a play.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: Lesley Manville and Mark Strong in “Oedipus.”

item.title: ‘Liberation’ Wins the Tony for Best Play
item.description: The playwright Bess Wohl is the first American woman to win this award since Wendy Wasserstein won for “The Heidi Chronicles” in 1989.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.media:description: From left: Audrey Corsa, Susannah Flood, Irene Sofia Lucio and Adina Verson in “Liberation.”

item.title: June Squibb is one of the oldest actors ever to be nominated for a Tony.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: June Squibb in the play “Marjorie Prime,” which had a limited run last winter at the Helen Hayes Theater.

item.title: John Lithgow Wins the Tony for Best Actor in a Play
item.description: In “Giant,” Lithgow leans into monstrosity to play the famed author Roald Dahl.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:description: John Lithgow as Roald Dahl in “Giant” at the Music Box Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: A Landmark Tonys Win for the Trans Costume Designer Qween Jean
item.description: Qween Jean won for “Cats: The Jellicle Ball,” becoming the first openly transgender person to win a Tony Award, according to a publicist for the show.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: Qween Jean accepting the Tony Award for best costume design of a musical, for “Cats: The Jellicle Ball.”

item.title: Pink Is Hosting Tonight’s Tony Awards
item.description: The pop star, a former theater kid, will also perform on Sunday. Her theater-loving daughter approves of the gig.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pink arrives at the 79th Tony Awards before hosting the ceremony.

item.title: A middle-school teacher who’s sent students to Broadway wins excellence in education award.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Vinny Varsalona/Vinny Varsalona, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Freddie Hendricks, a teacher at Utopian Academy for the Arts in Ellenwood, Ga., also founded the Youth Ensemble of Atlanta.

item.title: Who is going to win tonight?
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

item.title: American Players Theater is receiving the regional theater award.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: Eric Ruby for The New York Times
item.media:description: Samantha Newcomb and Briana J. Resa, top center on the balcony, in a 2024 American Players Theater production of “Much Ado About Nothing.”

item.title: André Bishop, Jules Fisher and James Lapine to receive lifetime achievement awards.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.media:credit: Dolly Faibyshev for The New York Times, Jenny Anderson/Getty Images For Tony Awards Pro, Jemal Countess/Getty Images For Tony Award Prod
item.media:description: From left, the producer André Bishop, the lighting designer Jules Fisher and the book writer and director James Lapine.

item.title: 38 Tony nominees on the strangest skills they’ve picked up for a show.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

item.title: What’s onstage this summer?
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: A play inspired by the horror franchise “Paranormal Activity” makes its Broadway bow in August.

item.title: Tonys 2026 Takeaways: ‘Schmigadoon!’ and ‘Death of a Salesman’ Are Big Winners
item.description: The Broadway revival of “Salesman” led with six Tony Awards. “Schmigadoon!” won best new musical, and “Ragtime” and “Liberation” also took home major prizes.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

item.title: Here’s what to expect at tonight’s ceremony.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: The CBS telecast of the Tonys introduces the latest plays and musicals to a television audience of several million.

item.title: How to watch the Tony Awards.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Pink will host the 79th Tony Awards at Radio City Music Hall. The ceremony, which will be broadcast live on CBS and is available to stream on Paramount+, begins at 8 p.m. Eastern.

item.title: June Squibb, the ‘Foulest Mouth on Broadway’, Is Up for a Tony at 96
item.description: At 96, June Squibb is one of the oldest acting nominees in the history of the Tony Awards. Back in the 1960s, she was known for an entirely different distinction.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: June Squibb has received her first Tony nomination, for her featured role in “Marjorie Prime.”

item.title: How to Watch the 2026 Tony Awards
item.description: The ceremony, at Radio City Music Hall, will be broadcast on CBS starting at 8 p.m. Eastern, and livestreamed on Paramount+ for premium subscribers.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Pink will host the 79th Tony Awards at Radio City Music Hall. The ceremony, which will be broadcast live on CBS and is available to stream on Paramount+, begins at 8 p.m. Eastern.

item.title: 2026 Tony Awards: What to Expect
item.description: This year’s annual celebration of the best on Broadway is being hosted by Pink.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: The CBS telecast of the Tonys introduces the latest plays and musicals to a television audience of several million.

item.title: ‘The Fantasticks’ and ‘Gloria’ Will Head to Broadway This Season
item.description: The nonprofit Second Stage Theater said it would present a reimagined version of “The Fantasticks” and the Pulitzer finalist “Gloria” at the Helen Hayes Theater.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: The 2015 production of Branden Jacobs-Jenkins’s play “Gloria” at the Vineyard Theater.

item.title: A New Play, ‘Kenrex,’ Revisits the Story of a Bully’s Unsolved Murder
item.description: A man was shot dead surrounded by witnesses in Skidmore, Mo., but no one was ever prosecuted. Now that act of vigilante justice has inspired the play “Kenrex.”
item.dc:creator: Jesse McKinley
item.media:description: The spot where Kenneth Rex McElroy was fatally shot, in broad daylight, outside the D & G Tavern in Skidmore, Mo.

item.title: ‘Jerome’ Review: Darkness Swallows a Three-Way Romance
item.description: John J. Caswell’s triangular romance set in the early 1990s speaks to us from the smoking psychic caldera left by AIDS.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: Sting, Billy Porter and Others Jazz Up June on Off Broadway Stages
item.description: Billy Porter, Wayne Brady, Sting and Suzan-Lori Parks are all slated to star on Off Broadway stages this month.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.category: Heated Rivalry: The Unauthorized Musical Parody (Play)

item.title: Andy Halliday, a Star of ‘Vampire Lesbians of Sodom,’ Dies at 73
item.description: A beloved member of Charles Busch’s Theater in Limbo repertory, he had an irrepressibly comic stage presence that masked a shy, tender disposition.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: ‘Awake and Sing!’ Heads to Broadway With Danny Burstein and Jessica Hecht
item.description: Danny Burstein, Jessica Hecht and Jeremy Shamos will star in the Manhattan Theater Club production of Clifford Odets’s 1935 play.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Angela Weiss/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images, Dia Dipasupil/Getty Images, Dia Dipasupil/Getty Images
item.media:description: From left: Danny Burstein, Jessica Hecht and Jeremy Shamos. Hecht, a three-time Tony nominee, will play Bessie Berger, the family matriarch of “Awake and Sing!”

item.title: What’s On in London Theaters This Summer
item.description: Catherine Tate, Greek classics and plenty in between — here’s our selection of West End productions for Londoners and visitors to check out over the coming months.
item.dc:creator: Matt Wolf
